text: This is the formation of a cock pouch or continent ileostomy in a sixty four year old man who had had rectal cancer and had previously undergone a robotic TME and ileostomy. He later developed local recurrence which was managed with an abdominal perineal excision and end colostomy but unfortunately he suffered from several stoma complications and was keen to have a continent stoma with the benefits of no longer necessitating wearing a stoma bag and reduced ileostomy complications.

The surgeon approaches the abdomen via a midline laparotomy incision, revealing the previous side-to-side stapled anastomosis from the ileostomy closure. Mesenteric vessels are sealed and divided using a ligature, and the terminal ileum is stapled and divided just proximal to the ileocecal valve. The surgeon measures fifteen centimeters from the transected terminal ileum to ensure sufficient small bowel length for the formation of the nipple valve and continent ileostomy. Additional measurements are taken for the three adjacent limbs of the S pouch, with sutures marking the top of the pouch and each limb to ensure equal length.

The surgeon positions the first and second, and second and third limbs of the small bowel adjacent to each other, joining them with a continuous 3-0 vicryl seromuscular suture. Diathermy is used to mark incision lines along each limb on either side of the suture line. The small bowel is carefully incised along these lines and laid open, with each limb of the pouch opened in turn, ensuring the posterior wall of the lumen and sutures are not damaged, especially when turning corners.

The surgeon sutures the adjacent posterior walls of the opened ileum loops together using a continuous three ovicle full thickness stitch, forming the posterior wall of the pouch. The anterior wall is then formed by suturing the lateral edges of the first and third limbs of the S pouch, with stay sutures placed at each end and in the middle of the anterior wall. Only the lower half of the pouch is joined at this stage, leaving the top half open for nipple valve formation.

The surgeon uses diathermy to thin out mesenteric fat bordering the terminal ileum, facilitating the interception for nipple valve formation. A diathermy scratch pad is applied to the serosal surface of the terminal ileum to promote adhesion between loops of interseceptive bowel. Doxycycline powder is rubbed onto the terminal ileum to irritate visceral layers and enhance adhesion. The peritoneum over the mesentery is incised at the site for the nipple valve, and attempts are made to intercept the ileum, with challenges noted due to bulky mesentery and narrow bowel lumen.

The surgeon successfully intercepts the terminal ileum with careful traction, forming the interception. A soft silastic drain is inserted from the distal ileum through the nipple valve into the top of the pouch. The rolled edge of the ileum is sutured with interrupted serosal sutures. A Reticulator-Fifty-Five stapler with a green 4.8 mm cartridge is used to place a double staggered row of titanium staples, fired at three points around the inter-suscepted bowel. The top of the anterior wall of the pouch is closed around the nipple valve with a series of interrupted sutures to minimize leak risk.

The surgeon places interrupted sutures to close the top of the pouch and along the anterior wall, anchoring the nipple valve within the pouch. The reticulator staple is inserted into the pouch and nipple valve, securing the valve to the anterior wall. The drain is withdrawn, and the pouch is tested for leaks and capacity by filling with normal saline. The patency of the nipple valve is verified through repeated catheterization and drainage checks.

The surgeon inserts a second layer of interrupted sutures along the anterior anastomosis using Threoethabond. A small skin incision, measuring 1.5 to 2 centimeters, is made in the lower right iliac fossa and extended through the anterior abdominal wall. An Alexis wound protector is placed to aid in the exteriorization of the terminal ileum. The pouch is checked for correct orientation and to ensure it is not twisted. The drain is used to confirm the patency of the nipple valve, and the pouch is filled with normal saline. The ileostomy is re-catheterized to verify adequate drainage.

The surgeon confirms the continence of the ileostomy by removing the drain when the pouch is full. Excess ileum is excised, and the ileostomy is formed flush with the skin, secured with interrupted vial stitches. The drain is positioned through the ileostomy, valve, and into the pouch, secured with a silk stitch. The operation concludes with a colectomy and closure of the previous colostomy site.
